turns.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="512" height="512" viewBox="0 0 512 512">
  <defs>
    <radialGradient id="g" cx="50%" cy="50%" r="50%">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffd54d"/>
      <stop offset="60%" stop-color="#ff8a65"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#d84315"/>
    </radialGradient>
  </defs>
  <rect width="100%" height="100%" fill="#0b1226"/>
  <g transform="translate(256,256)">
    <ellipse rx="160" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#90caf9" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.9"/>
    <ellipse rx="160" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#90caf9" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.9" transform="rotate(60)"/>
    <ellipse rx="160" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#90caf9" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.9" transform="rotate(-60)"/>
    <circle cx="160" cy="0" r="10" fill="#ffe082" stroke="#fff3e0" stroke-width="2"/>
    <g transform="rotate(60)">
      <circle cx="160" cy="0" r="10" fill="#fff59d" stroke="#fffde7" stroke-width="2"/>
    </g>
    <g transform="rotate(-60)">
      <circle cx="160" cy="0" r="10" fill="#ffe082" stroke="#fff3e0" stroke-width="2"/>
    </g>
    <circle cx="0" cy="0" r="28" fill="url(#g)"/>
    <circle cx="-12" cy="-8" r="14" fill="#ffb74d" opacity="0.9"/>
    <circle cx="14" cy="10" r="10" fill="#ffcc80" opacity="0.95"/>
    <ellipse rx="180" ry="90" fill="none" stroke="#81d4fa" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.07"/>
  </g>
</svg>

messages.content:
You are a drawing agent in a one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: svg.

Rules:
1) Draw the target word using only SVG graphics.
2) Output one complete <svg>...</svg> string.
3) Do not include text labels, letters, or numbers in the drawing.
4) Keep the SVG simple and valid. No scripts, no foreignObject.
5) Use a 512x512 canvas.

Output schema:
{ "svg": "string" }

The nucleus will consist of small circles with a radial gradient, and I'll need to compute the coordinates carefully for placing the electrons. Keeping it simple is key!**Creating elliptical orbits**\n\nI’m going to create elliptical orbits for the electrons. The first ellipse will have a rotation of 0, the second will be rotated 60 degrees, and the third will be at -60 degrees. {
  "svg": "<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="512" height="512" viewBox="0 0 512 512">

      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="#ffd54d"/>
      <stop offset="60%" stop-color="#ff8a65"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="#d84315"/>
    </radialGradient>
  </defs>
  <rect width="100%" height="100%" fill="#0b1226"/>
  <g transform="translate(256,256)">
    <ellipse rx="160" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#90caf9" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.9"/>
    <ellipse rx="160" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#90caf9" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.9" transform="rotate(60)"/>
    <ellipse rx="160" ry="80" fill="none" stroke="#90caf9" stroke-width="3" opacity="0.9" transform="rotate(-60)"/>
    <circle cx="160" cy="0" r="10" fill="#ffe082" stroke="#fff3e0" stroke-width="2"/>
    <g transform="rotate(60)">
      <circle cx="160" cy="0" r="10" fill="#fff59d" stroke="#fffde7" stroke-width="2"/>
    </g>
    <g transform="rotate(-60)">
      <circle cx="160" cy="0" r="10" fill="#ffe082" stroke="#fff3e0" stroke-width="2"/>
    </g>
    <circle cx="0" cy="0" r="28" fill="url(#g)"/>
    <circle cx="-12" cy="-8" r="14" fill="#ffb74d" opacity="0.9"/>
    <circle cx="14" cy="10" r="10" fill="#ffcc80" opacity="0.95"/>

message.reasoning:
**Creating SVG for atom**

I'm figuring out how to produce an SVG drawing that represents an "atom" using simple shapes, like a nucleus sphere and electron orbit rings with small circles for electrons. I need to make sure there's no text or numbers and that the canvas is 512x512. As I create the JSON output, I must remember to properly escape quotes. In JSON, the string value will need to have double quotes escaped. It's a bit tricky, but I’ll make it work!**Crafting SVG string**

I'm working on creating the SVG string for the atom design, making sure to escape double quotes correctly. The SVG will have a central nucleus made of three overlapping circles, possibly with a gradient, but no text. I plan to design three elliptical electron orbits around the nucleus, each at different angles, with strokes and no fill. The nucleus will consist of small circles with a radial gradient, and I'll need to compute the coordinates carefully for placing the electrons. Keeping it simple is key!**Creating elliptical orbits**

I’m going to create elliptical orbits for the electrons. The first ellipse will have a rotation of 0, the second will be rotated 60 degrees, and the third will be at -60 degrees. To place the electrons, I can compute their coordinates using transformations. To simplify things, instead of very complex calculations, I’ll just place small circles at the cardinal points along the ellipses. I can even compute the rightmost points based on their rotations. This should make things much easier!**Calculating electron positions**

I'm calculating the positions of the electrons based on their angles. For 0 degrees, I’ve found one electron at (416, 256). For 60 degrees, the coordinates are around (336, 394.56), and for -60 degrees, it’s (336, 117.44). I realize this creates symmetry with two electrons on the right and one on the left, so adding one more to make it triangular would be ideal. It would balance the placement and add to the visual effect!**Crafting SVG JSON**

I'm preparing to produce an SVG that shows three electron orbits, each represented as ellipses in different rotations (0, 60, and -60 degrees). The nucleus will consist of three concentric overlapping circles with a radial gradient. I’ll include smaller circles for the electrons and dotted lines for orbit paths. Now, I need to format the entire SVG string into JSON, ensuring I properly escape double quotes and newlines to avoid any issues. Once I have that ready, I'll output the final JSON!

messages.content:
You are the guesser in a visual one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: guesses.

Rules:
1) You receive one JPEG image of a drawing.
2) Return exactly 20 one-word guesses in order of confidence (best guess first).
3) The order is important and will be scored by first correct position.
4) Do not repeat guesses.
5) Each guess must be EN-US one word, no spaces.
6) Avoid punctuation and numbers.

Output schema:
